texts.full_text: nt option in patients with progressive local or metastatic disease. We constructed tissue microarrays from paraffin blocks prepared from 47 cases of non-medullary thyroid carcinomas and related normal thyroid tissue. Immunohistochemical staining was performed with five different polyclonal SSTR antibodies. SSTR subtypes sst2 and sst3 were expressed in all non-medullary thyroid carcinomas, sst1 and sst5 in 75%, and sst4 in 38%. Coexpression of more than three subtypes was detected in 36 of the 47 cases. The expression of SSTR subtypes in normal thyroid tissue was low or absent. Non-medullary thyroid carcinomas frequently express all SSTR subtypes. This expression provides a basis for further studies with

texts.full_text: with either acetic acid (\(\ce{HOAc}\)) or acetate ion (from deprotonation, denoted as AcO\(^-\)), associates to form an intimate carbocation-anion pair. This association is so fast that it can be considered complete within the dead time of stopped-flow measurements. The slow collapse step involves this tightly bound \( \text{TMT}(^+)-\)AcO\( ^- \) ion-pair transitioning into a neutral molecule, specifically trimethoxytrityl acetate (TMT-OAc), through covalent bond formation. This rate-determining process is independent of the concentration of added base (\(\ce{NaOAC}\)) or quaternary ammonium salts like tetra-n-butylammonium ion \((\text{(Bu}_4N^+)\) because it occurs after all TMT\( ^+\) has already paired with AcO(-). The independence from the concentration of acetate and other counterions indicates that these species do not directly participate in or significantly affect this slow, rate-determining step. Instead, they influence earlier equilibrium steps lead
